Criterion: Three-dimensional learning targets are separate and explicit
What pass requires: The lesson states three distinct learning targets: one for science & engineering practices (what students will practice), one for disciplinary core ideas (what students will understand), one for crosscutting concepts (which crosscutting concept students will apply). Pass = three distinct bullets, each labeled with the dimension. Fail = one merged objective, or DCI-only targets.

Criterion: Phenomenon is observable and drives the lesson
What pass requires: The anchoring phenomenon is something students can directly observe or is a specific real-world event (9–12). It is posed as a question to explain, not as an illustration of a concept the teacher will then explain. Pass = phenomenon is specific and students are positioned as sense-makers. Fail = phenomenon is abstract, described rather than experienced, or used as a hook before teacher explanation.

Students gather data or evidence before the teacher or lesson reveals the scientific explanation. Pass = investigation phase comes before sensemaking discussion and claim/evidence/reasoning. Fail = teacher explains the mechanism first, then students 'confirm' it through activity.

Criterion: CCC is named and used by students not just cited in the plan
What pass requires: A specific crosscutting concept (Patterns, Cause and Effect, Energy and Matter, etc.) is named in the lesson and students are required to use it explicitly — in their claim/evidence/reasoning writing, model, or discussion. Pass = CCC named and required in student output. Fail = CCC mentioned in teacher plan but students are never asked to apply it.

Criterion: Model revision is present and mechanistic
What pass requires: Students draw or update an explanatory model that shows how and why (mechanism), not just what (label). Model is revised based on evidence gathered in the lesson. Pass = model revision task present and described as mechanistic. Fail = model is a labeled diagram that predates the investigation, or no model revision step.

The claim/evidence/reasoning writing task requires students to cite specific evidence from the investigation (data, observations) — not from prior knowledge or the internet. Pass = prompt specifies 'from the investigation' or equivalent. Fail = students could write the CER without doing the investigation.

Criterion: Quantitative reasoning required in student analysis
What pass requires: For 6–12 standards with quantitative data: students must use numbers (not just qualitative descriptions) in their claims or explanations. Pass = at least one task requires numerical evidence or mathematical modeling. Fail = all analysis is qualitative when the data is quantitative.
Notes: Only applies to Gr 6–12 standards with quantitative data
